What about Atlas? I'd say imprint and fascination was the real biggie. Yeah. To our conversation at lunch, I mean, not really specific to this, but like what we talked about at the Info Summit, I mean, in the early years of the success movement in this country, they were all interlinked tightly. The health and fitness guys and the self-help guys, Napoleon Hill and the physical culture magazine, et cetera. Because essentially, they were all selling to the same people. And then it sort of started to splinter a little bit. But if you looked at, like for your business, I mean, Atlas's entire model has just been replicated again and again and again and again. Weeders done it consistently with one body after another, if you will. I mean, there's a template there. All right. Was the back end of the Atlas thing? Was it all fine? Very interesting. I almost bought that business. I tried to buy that business. There never was a back end, other than the List rental. And they made a crap load of money on List rental. The old man, Charles Roman, I got close. But they had what I considered to be an inflated value for what it was really worth. And it had to be an all cash deal. I urged Fury to go do it. I think Matt would have cleaned house with it. And it's back a little bit. That hoag guy you saw in there, this year, it's in, they got it in about 20 Christmas catalogs. And there's extra DVD with it and stuff. So they got the price point up to like $29 or $39. But nobody ever did a back end. And like, conversationally, like, you didn't want to hear about it. This is our business model. The only back end, I guess, if you will, is at some point they started to sell, which of course, Atlas was originally completely opposed to, they started to sell a dynamic tension device. So they did start to sell a essentially exercise bandy kind of thing. But that was the only product. There was never like a newsletter term, continuity didn't make any. There was never a continuing publication. There were never organized events. There were never any of that. And obviously, of course, I left a crap load of money on the table based on what we know now. The supplement business, of course, if you look at EAS or you look at Weeder, compared to these guys, the level of sophistication multiplied dramatically. This is for EAS products and stuff. Yeah. Yeah, the physical culture magazine's contest is the first that we know of. Bill doesn't know of one earlier, and I don't know of one earlier. Now, that doesn't mean there ain't one there somewhere. But it was huge for its time. And Bill's innovation, which was a really good innovation, and a lot of info marketers have missed it. I got Joe to do it originally. If Bill Phillips' innovation was, see the problem like when he said we got to kill the contest because Atlas will win it every year, say contests are no good if the vast majority of the people think they don't have a chance. And so Bill Phillips' innovation of the contest was the matrix of squares in which you compete against like competitors. So if you're a 40 to 50-year-old guy who's 25 pounds overweight or more, you're in this box with only other 40 to 50-year-old guys 25. Or if your goal is ab related, you're in only with ab related guys. If your goal is butt related, you're competing only with butt related people. And so he had 25, 30, 35 different quadrants. And we did that early with Joe's contest. And everybody, every other info marketer using it has basically been lazy about it and hasn't wanted to do it. But so all the secondary prizes are awarded to the winner of each little box. And then there's the one big prize for the best of the best of any of them. But there's a lot of secondary prizes. And there's the belief that you might be able to beat everybody just like you for one of these five prizes. And so that's really the only thing Phillips changed. And then Wider, of course, has had contests the entire time that he's been in this business. But again, Wider didn't do, he didn't do, break down the competition so you're only competing with people you think you can beat. That was, as far as I know, that was Bill Phillips' invention. But every time you see one of these things, then people who either aren't particularly interested in the history or don't spend any time on it, they think they're seeing something new. So people see Tony Robbins, they think they're seeing something new. They see a current magician. They see a copper field or making a plane disappear. They think they're seeing something radically new. And Christ goes back to the 1800s. I mean, so the smart guy, so like Phillips, he's like a pack rat in his industry like I am. I mean, he can go pull shit from 30, 40, 50 years ago and has a good understanding of how this thing produced this thing, which produced this thing, which produced this thing. And it's always building from that bank of all that information. And that's sort of what keeps you from missing a base. Because if you're only looking at the most contemporary stuff, you're seeing what's derivative of all these other steps. But it's kind of changed along the way. And somebody may have left out two of the magic ingredients or one of the magic ingredients. And the thing may still work, but it would work even better if you considered all of the ingredients. So, oh, wanted to, this is the Houdini book, for those of you that are readers, it's fabulous. What caught my eye was the subtitle is the Making of America's First Superhero. And it's The Secret Life of Houdini. And it authors a collusion, Slauman. And it's really, really good. It's a little slow going early. But that's out of the power book. Yeah. Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. And it and True Believer are probably the two sort of roadmaps to cults that are easily and readily accessible. You know, the Houdini book, when you talk about mental imprint, he makes the point. I mean, if you think about this, if we go to a coffee shop today and say, name a magician, eight out of 10 people, the first thing out of their mouth is still going to be Houdini. And this is after 80 years. I mean, and after a whole lot of stuff that has come and gone, you know, since. So he got that part of the deal right. So if you're a reader, I recommend the book. I'm into four chapters. And it's pretty darn good. OK, we are on page 128, Principle 19. And this is called the Principle of Self-Identification, which essentially means that the person quickly identifies they're in the right place. And they get it without being told. So it's not like blatant, like me holding up a sign that says you're in the right place and telling you that you're in the right place. But based on what is being said, what they are reading, their heart starts to beat a little faster. And their eyes start to dilate because they discover someone saying what they already believe. And maybe it's the first time or the first time in a long time they've bumped into somebody saying what they already believe, describing their experiences exactly the way they have it. Sort of the what I've heard on more than one occasion from my salespeople is it's like you're hiding under our kitchen table at midnight when we're having that discussion in the kitchen. And you overheard it. And you got it just right. And you're feeding it back to us. Well, yeah, that's sort of the point.
